content: Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) is a retrovirus that infects CD4+ T lymphocytes, leading to progressive immune dysfunction. HIV-1 causes most global infections; HIV-2 is less pathogenic and concentrated in West Africa. Without treatment, HIV progresses through acute infection (flu-like illness 2-4 weeks post-exposure), chronic asymptomatic phase (years), and AIDS (CD4 <200 cells/μL or AIDS-defining conditions). Antiretroviral therapy (ART) suppresses viral replication, preserves immune function, and prevents transmission. U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable): individuals with sustained undetectable viral load (<200 copies/mL) cannot sexually transmit HIV, based on HPTN 052, PARTNER, and Opposites Attract studies.
